goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

full_morning_briefing.definition: Evaluate the morning_message together with the same-day generated_actions, because both are shown to the user as one morning briefing experience.
full_morning_briefing.morning_message:
Hey Ameena,

You said you're feeling pretty good this morning (74) with high energy — that's awesome. Last night's sleep was critically short, just under 5 hours, which is about 1.5 hours less than your usual. Even though the sleep itself was efficient and you woke up late, that short duration is a real deficit. Your body's recovery score was low at 43, and your HRV is on a declining trend, which tells me your system didn't get the deep repair it needed.

What's interesting — and really strong — is how you're handling it. Your mood was high (74), stress was very low (17), and your coping capacity scored 84. That's a 31-point gap between how you feel and what your physiology shows, and it's been happening for three days now. You're clearly resilient, but that gap also means the strain from short sleep and yesterday's zero activity might be accumulating underneath the surface.

Yesterday was extremely sedentary (over 12 hours) with no real movement. That pattern, combined with the late sleep schedule, creates a phase-delayed, low-energy loop. The good news is your nutrition logging improved, and you kept caffeine timed well.

Today is about gently breaking that loop. The focus is on creating small, deliberate pauses to offset the sedentary inertia and protecting your sleep window tonight, especially with your Ramadan schedule.

You've got the mental bandwidth — today's about using it to give your body a chance to catch up.

generated_actions.title: Set a 'stop eating' alarm for 90 minutes before your target bedtime
fallbacks: If you miss the exact time, set it for the next night.
generated_actions.rationale: Last night's sleep was critically short (4.97h) with a very late schedule (onset at 5:31 AM). Your Ramadan eating pattern includes late snacks; creating a clear pre-sleep fasting window can improve digestion and sleep onset. This directly addresses the 'sleep schedule consistency' priority and 'avoid sleep debt accumulation' hard constraint.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you set and honor the 'stop eating' alarm 90 minutes before your planned bedtime?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports setting the alarm and stopping food/drink intake (except water) after it sounded, aiming for an earlier, more consistent sleep onset.

assumptions: You have a smartphone or device for alarms
You have a target bedtime in mind
generated_actions.description: Tonight, set an alarm on your phone for 90 minutes before the time you plan to go to sleep. When it goes off, finish your last snack or drink (water is fine) and do not consume anything else except sips of water.

generated_actions.title: After your first work block, stand up and take 5 deep breaths looking out a window
fallbacks: If you don't have a window, step outside your door or look down a long hallway.
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's sedentary time was extremely high (725 min, ~12 hours) with zero MVPA, contributing to low physiological recovery. This tiny, habit-stacked action disrupts prolonged sitting, resets visual focus, and introduces a deliberate pause to combat the 'volatility' and 'low_recovery' risk flags amid your 'tight schedule' and 'high workload'.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take a standing window-breath break after your first work session?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports intentionally pausing after work to stand, look into the distance, and take 5 deep breaths.

assumptions: You have access to a window or outdoor view
You will have a work block
generated_actions.description: When you finish your first concentrated work session today, immediately stand up, walk to a window, and take 5 slow, deep breaths while looking at the farthest point you can see.

must_avoid: checking your phone during the break

generated_actions.title: Do 3 minutes of gentle leg stretches after your evening prayer/ritual
generated_actions.rationale: Physiological recovery is below your norm (proxy score 43, HRV declining) after a day of zero MVPA and high sedentary time. This low-effort movement promotes blood flow and muscle relaxation before bed, supporting physical recovery and potentially improving sleep onset—addressing the 'low_recovery' risk flag and today's 'recovery_focus' priority.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you do 3 minutes of gentle leg stretches after your evening ritual?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing brief, gentle leg stretching as part of their wind-down routine.

assumptions: You engage in an evening prayer or ritual
You can safely perform gentle leg stretches
generated_actions.description: Right after you complete your evening prayer or winding-down ritual tonight, spend 3 minutes doing slow, gentle hamstring and calf stretches while standing or sitting on the edge of your bed.

generated_actions.title: When you get into bed, do 4 rounds of 4-7-8 breathing before touching your phone
fallbacks: If 4 rounds feel long, do 2 rounds.
generated_actions.rationale: Despite excellent sleep efficiency (93.4%), your sleep was severely truncated (under 5 hours). Your stress is very low (17) and coping high (84), making this a good time to cement a wind-down habit. This implementation intention uses your low stress to build a pre-sleep ritual that protects sleep opportunity and addresses the 'sleep_debt' risk flag.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you complete 4 rounds of 4-7-8 breathing before using your phone in bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports performing the breathing exercise immediately upon getting into bed, prior to any screen use.

generated_actions.description: As soon as you get into bed tonight, lie down and complete 4 rounds of 4-7-8 breathing (inhale 4 seconds, hold 7, exhale 8) before you reach for your phone or any other screen.

must_avoid: checking your phone before completing the breathing

assumptions: User is observing Ramadan (suhoor/iftar meals present)
6am basketball practices are scheduled midweek
High subjective scores (74) may not reflect physiological strain (physio_proxy_score = 43)
Missing goals data does not indicate goal abandonment
Journal missingness does not indicate absence of stressors

evidence_used.meal_context: ramadan_suhoor, ramadan_iftar, snack with iced latte

soft_constraints: follow up on missing goals data
prioritize sleep schedule consistency (sleep duration 4.97h below 6h goal)
caution with evening caffeine (iced latte logged as snack)
promote recovery after low sleep efficiency night

previous_day_generated_actions.title: When you start your first work block, set a 45-minute timer and hide your phone
fallbacks: If 45 minutes feels too long, start with a 25-minute timer.
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Your focus score (37) remains low despite high mood (77) and energy (76), with a focus-energy gap of -39. This structured, time-bound action directly tackles the 'monitor focus-energy mismatch' soft constraint by creating a distraction-free environment to channel your high subjective energy into cognitive work.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you set a 45-minute timer and put your phone away for your first work block?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports setting the timer and avoiding phone use for the duration, aiming to improve today's focus score from yesterday's 37.

assumptions: You have access to a timer (computer, watch, or separate device)
Your work does not critically require phone access for the next 45 minutes
previous_day_generated_actions.description: Right as you begin your first focused work session, set a 45-minute countdown timer on your computer or a separate device. Place your smartphone face down or in another room until the timer goes off.

must_avoid: checking your phone during the work block

previous_day_generated_actions.title: At 3 PM, stand up and do 10 slow shoulder rolls
fallbacks: If you miss 3 PM, do it at the next natural break.
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's sedentary time was high (637 min) amid a 'tight schedule' and 'high workload'. This tiny, time-anchored action introduces a deliberate physical pause to disrupt prolonged sitting, promote bodily awareness, and support stability against the 'volatility' risk flag, leveraging your low stress (12) to make it easy to adopt.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you stand up and do 10 slow shoulder rolls at 3 PM?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing the brief movement break at the specified time.

assumptions: You will be at a desk or seated environment in the afternoon
You can safely perform gentle shoulder movements
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When your clock hits 3:00 PM this afternoon, stand up from your desk and perform 10 slow, deliberate shoulder rolls (5 forward, 5 backward). Focus on the movement and your breath.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: At iftar tonight, add one extra spoon of a protein source to your plate
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's protein intake was low (35.1g) and on a steep downward trend. Your iftar description included beef kidney stew—this action builds on that by making a tiny, specific adjustment to gently boost protein within your Ramadan pattern, addressing the 'nutrition_gap' risk flag while respecting improved logging confidence (0.70).

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you add an extra spoon of protein to your iftar meal?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports intentionally increasing the protein portion of their evening meal.

assumptions: You are observing Ramadan and will have an iftar meal
Protein sources are available
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you break your fast this evening, consciously add one more spoonful of a protein-rich food (like the beef kidney stew, yogurt, or eggs) to your meal before you start eating.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: After your evening prayer/ritual, dim the main lights
fallbacks: If you don't have dimmable lights, turn off some lights and use a smaller lamp.
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Last night's sleep was long but inefficient (82.9%) with elevated wake time (17.1%). Habit-stacking light reduction onto an existing evening routine supports melatonin production and sleep onset, directly targeting sleep efficiency. This respects your '6am basketball practices midweek' constraint by promoting earlier, higher-quality wind-down.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you dim the main lights after your evening ritual?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports reducing ambient light intensity for at least 60 minutes before bed.

assumptions: You have control over your lighting environment
You engage in an evening prayer or winding-down routine
previous_day_generated_actions.description: Immediately after completing your evening prayer or winding-down ritual tonight, turn off or dim the main overhead lights. Use only lamps, nightlights, or natural light for the next 60 minutes.

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Morning, Ameena. You said you're feeling great this morning with an 86 — that's high energy and a strong start.

Let's look at last night first: you got **9.3 hours** of sleep, which is way above your usual. That's a huge win for recovery time. Your HRV was strong at 112ms and you had good deep sleep, so your body got some solid repair work done. The catch? You were awake for 17% of that time, so it was a bit fragmented — maybe not as restful as it could have been.

Yesterday, your mood was **77** and stress was only **12**, which is fantastic. But here's the interesting thing: your focus score was 37, which is low compared to that high energy. It's like your brain wasn't fully tapping into the good vibes. Also, you barely moved yesterday — only 4,444 steps — while your body was in full recovery mode. That's a unusual combo: your system was ready for more, but you stayed still.

The big story today is about **channeling this high subjective energy into your cognitive work** while honoring your body's recovery. You've got the mood and low stress to support a focused day; it's about directing that fuel.

Your actions today are designed to bridge that gap — turning your great mood into tangible focus and maintaining the stability you've built.

With your stress this low and sleep that long, you have a real opportunity to make today both productive and grounded.

evidence_quotes: It's been a while since I've been able to sit and focus on work
went out eith my grandma, mom and sister for some coffee which was heart warming

meal_descriptions.description: One egg omelet and 4 tablespoons of fava beans with tomato and an Arabic bread loaf
2 cheese sambosak and 2 thin beef slices and 2 spoons of rice with peas
An iced latte and a marble cake slice
2 thin beef slices with mustard sauce
